source_intake_record.raw_question_text: Assume all surfaces to be frictionless and the inertia of pulley and cord negligible (Fig. $1.6$). Find the horizontal force necessary to prevent any relative motion of $m_{1}$, $m_{2}$ and $M$.

requires_image=true and text_dominant=false indicate the image is central rather than optional
the arrangement of m1, m2, M, pulley, and force direction is not fully specified in text alone
multi_step: the condition 'prevent any relative motion of m1, m2 and M' implies simultaneous constraints across multiple bodies
frictionless surfaces and negligible pulley/cord inertia suggest a mechanics derivation with multiple linked force/acceleration relations
the final answer depends on m1, m2, M, and g, consistent with combining several conditions rather than a single direct lookup
verifiability: answer_type is short_text and the provided answer is a closed-form symbolic expression
registry_passed=true, answer_present=true, and question_present=true support downstream checking
the expression can be verified by symbolic equivalence or by re-deriving the force balance from the stated setup

candidate_problem_record.raw_question_text: Assume all surfaces to be frictionless and the inertia of pulley and cord negligible (Fig. $1.6$). Find the horizontal force necessary to prevent any relative motion of $m_{1}$, $m_{2}$ and $M$.
candidate_problem_record.multi_solution_policy_rationale: 该数据集保留多解潜力评估，但不默认强推多解 agent。

extraction_notes: images fields contain PIL object placeholder strings rather than actual file paths or URLs
caption content appears inconsistent with the question text
metadata.reasoning_chain: The forces $f_{1}, F$ and $m g$ are shown in Fig. $1.7$. The accelerations of $m_{1}, m_{2}$ and $M$ are the same when there is no relative motion among them. The equations of motion along the $x$-axis are $$\begin{array}{c} \left(M+m_{1}+m_{2}\right) \ddot{x}=F \\ m_{1} \ddot{x}=f_{1} \end{array}$$ As there is no relative motion of $m_{2}$ along the $y$-axis, $$f_{1}=m_{2} g$$ Combining these equations, we obtain $$F=\frac{m_{2}\left(M+m_{1}+m_{2}\right) g}{m_{1}}$$

problem_main_record.reasoning_chain: The forces $f_{1}, F$ and $m g$ are shown in Fig. $1.7$. The accelerations of $m_{1}, m_{2}$ and $M$ are the same when there is no relative motion among them. The equations of motion along the $x$-axis are $$\begin{array}{c} \left(M+m_{1}+m_{2}\right) \ddot{x}=F \\ m_{1} \ddot{x}=f_{1} \end{array}$$ As there is no relative motion of $m_{2}$ along the $y$-axis, $$f_{1}=m_{2} g$$ Combining these equations, we obtain $$F=\frac{m_{2}\left(M+m_{1}+m_{2}\right) g}{m_{1}}$$
